---
title: "lab4"
format: html
editor: visual
---

1 and 2

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
data <- read.csv("C:/Users/57146/Downloads/covid_processed.csv")
data <- data %>%
  mutate(date = ymd(date))
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    cases_rate   = cases   / pop * 100000,
    hosp_rate    = hosp    / pop * 100000,
    booster_rate = booster / pop * 100000,
    series_rate  = series  / pop * 100000,
    deaths_rate  = deaths  / pop * 100000
  )
state_avg <- data %>%
  group_by(state, state_name) %>%
  summarise(
    pop          = first(pop),
    region       = first(region),
    region_name  = first(region_name),
    cases_rate   = mean(cases_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    hosp_rate    = mean(hosp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    booster_rate = mean(booster_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    series_rate  = mean(series_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    deaths_rate  = mean(deaths_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

med_pop <- median(state_avg$pop, na.rm = TRUE)
state_avg <- state_avg %>%
  mutate(bi_pop = ifelse(pop > med_pop, "Large", "Small"))

state_avg <- state_avg %>%
  mutate(
    region = as.factor(region),
    bi_pop = factor(bi_pop, levels = c("Small", "Large"))
  )

state_avg

```

3

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)

ggplot(state_avg, aes(x = region_name, y = cases_rate, fill = region_name)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7, color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Case Rates by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Cases per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1)
  )


ggplot(state_avg, aes(x = region_name, y = deaths_rate, fill = region_name)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7, color = "grey30") +
  labs(
    title = "Death Rates by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Deaths per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1)
  )
##Case rates vary across regions, with some regions such as the Mountain States and Southeast showing higher typical levels. Other regions, such as the New York Islands and Pacific, show lower or more variable case rates.

##Death rates vary across by regions, like southeast region and south central showing higher typical levels. While 

```

4

```{r}
start_date <- min(data$date)

data_after2weeks <- data[data$date >= start_date + 14, ]
end_date <- start_date + 14

data_2weeks <- data[data$date <= end_date, ]

ggplot(data_after2weeks, aes(x = date, y = cases_rate, color = region_name)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  stat_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Case Rates Over Time by Region",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Cases per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(data_after2weeks,
       aes(x = date, y = cases_rate, color = region_name)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.8) +
  stat_smooth(se = FALSE, span = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Case Rates Over Time by Region",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Cases per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()



## The plot shows a clear increase in case rates during late 2020, followed by a decline and another rise toward the end of 2021. Although the general trend is similar across regions, the magnitude of case rates differs, with some regions experiencing higher peaks than others.
```

5

```{r}
ggplot(state_avg, aes(x = bi_pop, fill = region_name)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Number of States by Population Category and Region",
    x = "Population Category",
    y = "Number of States",
    fill = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)


##The barplot shows the number of states is not evenly distributed between population groups across regions.
```

6

```{r}
ggplot(data, aes(x = region_name, y = series_rate, fill = region_name)) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = "mean_sdl", geom = "bar") +
  stat_summary(fun.data = "mean_sdl", geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Vaccination Rate by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Vaccination Rate per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

ggplot(data, aes(x = region_name, y = deaths_rate, fill = region_name)) +
  stat_summary(fun.data = "mean_sdl", geom = "bar") +
  stat_summary(fun.data = "mean_sdl", geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Death Rate by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Death Rate per 100,000"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))


##The mean vaccination rate varies slightly across regions, with New England and New York Islands showing somewhat higher average coverage. However, the large error bars indicate substantial variability within regions, suggesting that differences among states within the same region may be greater than differences between regions.

## Mean death rates differ across regions, with South Central and Southeast exhibiting higher average death rates compared to other regions. The wide error bars indicate considerable variability in death rates within regions, reflecting heterogeneity across states.
```

7

```{r}

library(dplyr)

us_map <- map_data("state")
state_map_df <- state_avg %>%
  mutate(region = tolower(state_name)) %>%
  select(region, state_name, deaths_rate)
map_joined <- us_map %>%
  left_join(state_map_df, by = "region")
top10 <- state_avg %>%
  arrange(desc(deaths_rate)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  mutate(region = tolower(state_name)) %>%
  select(region, state_name, deaths_rate)
centers <- us_map %>%
  group_by(region) %>%
  summarise(
    long = mean(range(long)),
    lat  = mean(range(lat)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

top10_centers <- centers %>%
  inner_join(top10, by = "region")
ggplot(map_joined, aes(x = long, y = lat, group = group)) +
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = deaths_rate), color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_fixed(1.3) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = c("#f7fbff", "#c6dbef", "#6baed6", "#2171b5", "#08306b"),
    name = "Deaths per 100,000"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = top10_centers,
    aes(x = long, y = lat),
    label = "*",
    color = "black",
    size = 6,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Spatial Pattern of COVID Death Rates by State",
    subtitle = "Asterisks mark the 10 states with the highest death rates"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


##The map shows substantial spatial variation in COVID death rates across the United States. States in the Southeast and parts of the South Central region appear to have higher death rates, as indicated by darker shading and the concentration of asterisks marking the top 10 states. In contrast, several states in the West and Northeast exhibit relatively lower death rates. Overall, COVID mortality is not evenly distributed geographically, with clear regional clustering of higher death rates in the southern United States.
```

8

```{r}
library(ggforce)
ggplot(state_avg,
       aes(x = cases_rate, y = deaths_rate, color = region_name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_mark_ellipse(aes(fill = region_name),
                    alpha = 0.1,
                    show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Association Between Case Rate and Death Rate by State",
    x = "Mean Case Rate per 100,000",
    y = "Mean Death Rate per 100,000",
    color = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

```
